title: Choosing Stride & Padding for a Task
concept: Stride and padding aren't one-size-fits-all: stride 1 with same padding suits careful feature extraction, larger strides suit downsampling for efficiency, and valid padding suits tasks where exact, uncontaminated cropping matters.

exercises.prompt: Recap: On a 5x5 image with a 3x3 filter and 1-pixel padding, why does switching from stride 1 to stride 2 shrink the output even though the padding didn't change?

options.text: Padding only controls how much room the filter has to move in — stride still controls how many of those spots get used
Because padding gets removed automatically at stride 2
Because stride 2 is incompatible with padding

exercises.explain: Right — padding and stride are independent dials that combine, not substitutes for each other.

cards.title: So which settings do you actually pick?
cards.body: You now know what stride and padding each do. The real question every CNN designer faces next: given a specific task, which settings should you actually reach for?

cards.title: Three common goals, three common choices
cards.body: Feature extraction (learning rich detail): stride 1 with same padding. Downsampling (shrinking for speed, like a pooling layer): a larger stride, like 2. Exact cropping (no zero-padding artifacts allowed): valid padding.

cards.title: Picking the right tool, not the 'best' one
cards.body: Stride and padding are like a wrench and a screwdriver — neither is universally 'better.' You reach for stride 1 and same padding when a job needs careful, detailed work, and a bigger stride when the job just needs a fast, rough pass.

cards.title: Three tasks, three setups
cards.body: A face-detail network's early layers use stride 1 + same padding to keep every pixel of detail through many stacked layers. A network trying to shrink a huge 1000x1000 satellite image down efficiently uses stride 2 to cut the output roughly in half each layer, saving computation. A network cropping a document scan to exactly its printed content, with zero tolerance for fake zero-padding edges leaking in, uses valid padding.

cards.title: There's no single 'default' setting
cards.body: It's tempting to assume one combination is simply the industry standard for everything. Real networks mix and match: some layers use stride 1 with same padding to build up detail, then a later layer switches to stride 2 to downsample — all within the same model.

cards.title: Decision matrix
cards.body: Match the goal to the setting:

left.text:
Goal: feature extraction
Keeps full resolution
Maximizes detail per layer

right.text:
Goal: downsampling
Shrinks output on purpose
Saves computation

exercises.prompt: A CNN layer needs to extract fine detail from an image while keeping the spatial size constant across many stacked layers. Which stride and padding combination fits best?

options.text: Stride 1 with no padding at all

exercises.explain: Right — stride 1 keeps every position, and same padding keeps the output size from shrinking as it stacks through many layers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A larger stride, like stride 2, is a reasonable choice specifically when the goal is downsampling an image for speed, not preserving every fine detail.
exercises.explain: True — a larger stride intentionally skips positions, producing a smaller output faster, which is exactly what downsampling wants.

exercises.sentence: When exact cropping matters and no zero-padding artifacts should leak into the output, ___ padding is the right choice.
exercises.explain: Valid padding adds no zero border at all, so the output only ever reflects real, original pixels — ideal when exact cropping matters.

exercises.prompt: A team is building a fast mobile app that needs to shrink a large camera image down quickly before running heavier processing. Which choice best fits their goal?

options.text: A larger stride, like 2, to reduce the output size and computation in fewer steps
Stride 1 with same padding, to keep the image at full size throughout
Valid padding with stride 1, since padding style doesn't affect speed at all

exercises.explain: A larger stride directly cuts down the number of filter positions computed, which is exactly the speedup a mobile app downsampling step needs.

exercises.prompt: You're designing a CNN with three goals across different parts of the network: (1) early layers that preserve every detail of a medical scan, (2) a middle section that needs to shrink the image efficiently to save computation, and (3) a final layer that crops out an exact region with zero tolerance for fake padded edges. Match each goal to its best stride/padding choice.

options.text: (1) Stride 1 + same padding, (2) larger stride like 2, (3) valid padding
All three goals should use stride 1 with same padding, since that's always the safest choice
All three goals should use valid padding, since 'valid' is simply the correct default
(1) larger stride, (2) valid padding, (3) stride 1 with same padding

exercises.explain: Exactly right — stride 1 with same padding for detail-preserving layers, a larger stride for efficient downsampling, and valid padding when exact, artifact-free cropping matters. That's the real skill: matching the dial to the job. Next up: what these convolution outputs actually are — feature maps.
